title: Intact DNA purified from flow-sorted nuclei unlocks the potential of next-generation genome mapping and assembly in Solanum species
author: Gaiero, Šimková, et al.
materials: Keep the mixture at 52 ℃ for another 5 min and then slowly pour the mixture into pre-warmed plug molds (Bio-Rad, 1703713) using a wide bore pipette tip.

Push the plugs into a polystyrene tube with 500 μL per plug of lysis buffer C (0.5 M EDTA, 1% N-lauroyl-sarcosine (Sigma Aldrich, L5000)). Add freshly prepared proteinase K (Sigma Aldrich, P6556) to reach a final concentration of 0.3 mg/mL (30 μL of stock solution per 1 mL). For the two washes, prepare 62 μL of stock solution per 1 mL of buffer. Weigh 62 μg and dilute in 62 μL of MQ (milli-Q, Millipore Corporation) water. Before weighing, let the proteinase K adapt to room temperature to avoid condensation on the powder. Add 30 μL for the first wash and keep the rest at 4 ℃ for the second wash.

Change for lysis buffer B (0.5 M EDTA, 1% N-lauroyl-sarcosine (Sigma Aldrich, L5000), 0.3 mg mL−1 proteinase K (Sigma Aldrich, P6556, pH 8.0) and incubate plugs for another 24 h under the same conditions.

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: The whole workflow for this method is summarized in the graphical abstract. The method follows Šimková et al. with the following modifications. First of all, we introduced variations in the starting material types. For tomato (Solanum lycopersicum), we used root tips, for S. commersonii young leaves from in vitro plantlets and for S. etuberosum young leaves from plants grown in pots. Second, we tested different options to obtain nuclei suspensions, namely homogenizing with a Polytron or chopping with a razor blade. The protocol for tomato root tips is as follows:

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: Germinate about 200 seeds on humidified filter paper in Petri dishes for about 4 days.

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: Add 1.1 μL β-mercaptoethanol per 1 mL of 1.5x isolation buffer (IB [1]: 15 mM Tris, 10 mM EDTA, 130 mM KCl, 20 mM NaCl, 1 mM spermine, 1 mM spermidine and 0.1% Triton X-100, pH 9.4) just before use.

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: Transfer the seedlings to a 2% formaldehyde solution (from stock solution 36.5–38 % in H2O, SIGMA F8775) in Tris buffer (10 mM Tris, 10 mM EDTA, 100 mM NaCl, 0.1% v/v Triton X-100, pH 7.5). Incubate in a water bath at 4 ℃ for 20 min and wash three times in Tris buffer at 4 ℃ for 5 min.

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: Dissect 1–2 cm of the root tips on a glass Petri dish, divide the material between two 5 mL polystyrene tubes containing 1 mL ice-cold 1.5x IB with β-mercaptoethanol and keep on ice.

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: Homogenize samples using a Polytron PT1200 homogenizer at 15,000 rpm for 13 s (time and speed adjustable according to species).

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: The alternative protocol for S. commersonii and S. etuberosum young plant leaves is as follows:

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: Fix whole in vitro grown plantlets or detached leaves from potted plants in formaldehyde solution as described above.

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: After rinsing, place 0.5–1 g of leaves in a glass Petri dish with 1 mL of 1.5× IB buffer

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: Chop the tissues using a sharp razor blade until a soft homogenate is obtained. This should be formed by very small pieces of leaves in a green suspension. Continue from step 1.6.

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: Filter the crude homogenates through a 50 μm nylon mesh into a new polystyrene tube and a 25 μm nylon mesh (Silk & Progress, 130 T EXTRA, www.silkandprogress.cz), respectively. Alternatively, samples can be filtered through a Falcon® 40 μm cell strainer (Corning Life Sciences, Oneonta, New York, Product #352,340). Collect the filtered nuclei suspensions aliquots up to a volume of ∼4 mL.

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: Add DAPI to a final concentration of 2 μg mL−1. Check nuclei integrity and concentration under the fluorescence microscope equipped with appropriate excitation and emission filters. Nuclei should be round-shaped, not broken and at a density of 150–200 nuclei per mm2 (at 10× magnification).

steps.title: Plant material and preparation of nuclei suspensions (for two plugs with 500,000 nuclei each)
steps.instruction: Keep samples on ice until flow cytometric analysis and sorting.

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: Plug preparation was performed following Šimková et al. We modified the centrifugation steps (speed and time) to make them more efficient to recover Solanum nuclei.

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: Pellet nuclei (400,000–500,000 per tube) at 500 g and 4 ℃ for 30 min.

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: Two fluorescence microscopic checkpoints can be optionally introduced, before and after pelleting, for nuclei integrity and concentration after staining with 2 μg mL−1 DAPI or 50 μg mL−1 PI (Fig. 1b).

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: Discard supernatant keeping 15 μL in the tube with the pellet. Gently resuspend pelleted nuclei in the leftover supernatant.

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: Warm the nuclei suspension and keep at 52 ℃ for 3–5 min

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: Mix with 8.5 μL 2% low-melting point (LMP) agarose (Bio-Rad, 1613111) dissolved in 1x IB.

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: Solidify the plugs at 4 ℃ for 10 min

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: Incubate at 37 ℃ for 24 h under gentle shaking (50 rpm) in an almost horizontal position.

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: After the proteinase K treatment, rinse and store agarose plugs in ET buffer (1 mM Tris, 50 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) at 4 ℃.

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: Check DNA quality using pulsed field gel electrophoresis (e.g. CHEF-DR II system, Bio-Rad or BluePippin, Sage Science) (Fig. 2a and b).Fig. 2a. Quality check of Solanum lycopersicum HMW DNA (h) by PFGE. λ - Lambda Ladder, unit size 48.6 kb. b. PFGE of restriction enzyme digestion of Solanum commersonii HMW DNA for accessibility test (cropped image). YC- yeast chromosomes, Control - HMW DNA, 20 min at 37 ℃ in digestion buffer without restriction enzymes, HindIII and EcoRI, 2 U for 20 min at 37 ℃.Fig. 2

steps.title: Agarose plugs preparation and quality controls
steps.instruction: The plugs obtained were ready for RNAse treatment, DNA release and labeling following the standard protocol recommended by the genome mapping platform manufacturer (BioNano Genomics).

steps.title: Advantages over comparable methods
steps.instruction: Production of HMW DNA of superior quality, i.e., molecules of several hundred kilobases, has been identified as the bottleneck in nanochannel-based genome mapping technologies (BioNano Genomics). Our method yields the longest DNA molecules on average (290 kbp, compared to the 160 Kbp molecules and 7.5 sites per 100 Kbp obtained through other methods, unpublished results). Run costs become lower, as longer molecules mean that less coverage is needed when doing genome mapping. Moreover, longer molecules with proper labeling frequency imply higher N50 in the consensus genome map. Complex regions in the genome can be spanned and resolved better, which contributes to the contiguity of the assembly.

steps.title: Advantages over comparable methods
steps.instruction: Contamination by cytosol inclusions in different plant cell types represents another important impediment to the use of optical mapping. Such cytoplasmic compounds, mostly phenolic compounds, polysaccharides and other secondary metabolites co-precipitate with DNA and interfere with enzymes used in DNA labeling. When nuclei are flow-sorted, the contamination with such substances is minimized, and so ultimately leads to fewer false negatives in optical mapping. Purity also reduces clogging of nanochannels, thus allowing for higher throughput since it extends chip lifetime. In terms of cytoplasmic contaminants, flow sorting yields HMW DNA of much higher purity compared to other methods, without cell debris and very low amounts of chloroplast and mitochondria contamination [2], which could represent a problem because in large leaves the amount of chloroplast DNA is often much higher than that of nuclear DNA [3].

steps.title: Advantages over comparable methods
steps.instruction: The yield of pure, HMW DNA from the tomato cv Heinz 1706 material (genome size 950 Mbp) amounted about 1.6 × 106 nuclei in four agarose miniplugs, which was enough to produce 73 Gbp of size-filtered data with single molecule N50 of 290 Kbp on the Irys platform. We obtained this sample of nuclei in 3 h of sorting in a FACSAria or 6 h of sorting in a FACSVantage flow cytometer. These results confirm the excellent quality of nuclear plant DNA obtained through flow sorting which is similar to the quality obtained from mammalian cell cultures. Moreover, molecule size distribution was on par with human samples (personal communication, BioNano Genomics).
